title: The Drawer That Still Sticks
description: A narrator returns to the junk drawer in the kitchen of a house that no longer stands, and finds that the drawer still jams in memory exactly the way it did in real life.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
Third drawer down, left of the stove
It always stuck if you pulled it slow
You had to lift the handle and push it in first
Nobody taught me, I just knew the trick

[Verse 2]
Rubber bands, a tape measure, a key to no door
A church bulletin from nineteen-eighty-four
I'm not there anymore, the house isn't either
But my hand still remembers exactly where to go

[Chorus]
I'm drawing the drawer on a page in the atlas
I'm marking the spot with an X and a date
This is the country where nothing got finished
This is the drawer that still sticks, still sticks, still sticks

[Verse 3]
I used to think the maps were for places
Mountains and rivers and borders on lines
But some of the realest ground I have walked on
Was eighteen inches of kitchen drawer space

[Chorus]
I'm drawing the drawer on a page in the atlas
I'm marking the spot with an X and a date
This is the country where nothing got finished
This is the drawer that still sticks, still sticks, still sticks

[Bridge]
If I lift the handle and push it in first
If I do it exactly the way that I learned
Maybe it opens onto more than rubber bands and keys
Maybe it opens onto me

[Outro]
Third drawer down, left of the stove
I'm still the only one who knows

title: Paper Boats on the Gutter River
description: A memory of racing folded paper boats down a rain-flooded street gutter becomes a meditation on how far a small, ordinary joy can carry a person in their mind decades later.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
It rained on a Tuesday, the gutters ran full
We tore homework pages and folded them small
Yours had a math problem, mine had a spelling test
We never once asked whose boat would win

[Verse 2]
Mine caught on a leaf by the Petersons' drive
Yours made it all the way to the storm drain and dove
We stood in the wet grass and cheered like you'd won something real
Maybe we had, I still can't say what

[Chorus]
Paper boats on the gutter river
Going nowhere, going everywhere I've been
I didn't know yet that a road could be water
I didn't know a Tuesday could last this long

[Verse 3]
Now I draw rivers on paper for a living
Lines that go nowhere but down to a name
And every gutter after a hard rain
Still has your boat in it, still going down

[Chorus]
Paper boats on the gutter river
Going nowhere, going everywhere I've been
I didn't know yet that a road could be water
I didn't know a Tuesday could last this long

[Bridge]
Math problem, spelling test, sunk in a storm drain
That's the whole map of that afternoon
No treasure, no X, just two kids in the wet grass
Watching the only thing they ever set free

[Outro]
Going nowhere, going everywhere I've been
Still going down, down, down

title: The Globe with the Worn Pacific
description: An old desktop globe, its paint rubbed pale across the Pacific Ocean from years of small fingers spinning it, becomes a symbol of how touch and longing leave marks deeper than geography.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
The globe sat on the bookshelf, tilted on its axis wrong
We'd spin it with one finger and stop it with a thumb
Wherever it landed, that's where we were going
Most weeks it was always somewhere blue

[Verse 2]
The Pacific's gone pale now, the paint rubbed down to white
From a thousand small hands stopping it at night
Nobody painted that ocean to be touched that much
But that's the only color that wore off

[Chorus]
I'm mapping the places that never were real
I'm mapping the want more than the land
The globe doesn't turn anymore, the axis is stuck
But the white spot still says how much we wished

[Verse 3]
I used to think someday I'd go to all of it
Now I just keep the globe on my own shelf, dust and all
I don't spin it the same way, my thumb's too careful
I don't want to wear out the only proof

[Chorus]
I'm mapping the places that never were real
I'm mapping the want more than the land
The globe doesn't turn anymore, the axis is stuck
But the white spot still says how much we wished

[Bridge]
Pale ocean, worn smooth, no waves left in the paint
Just a thumbprint history, a faded blue saint
Of every place we said we'd go together
And never once needed to leave the room

[Outro]
Wherever it landed, that's where we were going
The white spot still spins in me

title: The Attic Stairs Have Fourteen Steps
description: Counting the fourteen creaking stairs to a childhood attic becomes a ritual the narrator still performs in memory, each step holding a different fragment of who lived in that house.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
One is the step where the wood always groaned
Two and three you could skip if you were brave
Four is where I sat the night the power went out
Five through nine I don't remember at all

[Verse 2]
Ten is crooked, it leans to the left
Eleven's where I left a coin for luck
Twelve and thirteen are close enough to touch the ceiling
Fourteen opens into all that dust and light

[Chorus]
Fourteen steps and I could climb them blind
Fourteen steps and half of them are mine
I'm drawing the staircase like a road on the atlas
Every step a country I forgot I had a key to

[Verse 3]
The house is somebody else's now, repainted, gutted, new
They probably tore the staircase out for something open-plan
But I can still feel twelve lean under my weight
I can still hear four go quiet in the dark

[Chorus]
Fourteen steps and I could climb them blind
Fourteen steps and half of them are mine
I'm drawing the staircase like a road on the atlas
Every step a country I forgot I had a key to

[Bridge]
One, two, skip, four — sit, breathe, wait for the lights
Five through nine, gone, gone, gone, gone, gone
Ten leans, eleven's lucky, twelve and thirteen low ceiling
Fourteen — and the dust, and the light, and the boxes nobody opened

[Outro]
Fourteen steps and I could climb them blind
I'm still climbing, I never left

title: The Room with the Taped-Up Window
description: A bedroom window cracked one winter and patched with packing tape becomes the first room on the map where the narrator senses something in the house was quietly, permanently wrong.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
The crack ran corner to corner, thin as a hair
Dad said he'd fix it in spring, then summer, then never
The tape went yellow before the next winter came
And nobody said the word that we were thinking

[Verse 2]
I used to trace the crack with my finger at night
Follow it like a road that went nowhere good
The cold came through anyway, tape or no tape
I learned to sleep in two sweaters and not ask why

[Chorus]
This is the room I don't put on the atlas
This is the room I draw in pencil, not ink
Because some of the cold in that house wasn't weather
And some of the cracks were never going to mend

[Verse 3]
We moved out before the glass ever got replaced
I think about the next kid in that room
Did the tape hold, did the cold still come through
Did they trace the same crack and wonder the same thing

[Chorus]
This is the room I don't put on the atlas
This is the room I draw in pencil, not ink
Because some of the cold in that house wasn't weather
And some of the cracks were never going to mend

[Bridge]
Spring, then summer, then never
That's not a season, that's a sentence
I was eight years old and already learning
How long a grown-up can let something stay broken

[Outro]
The tape went yellow, the crack stayed thin
I'm still tracing it, I'm still tracing it

title: The Hallway That Got Shorter
description: The narrator recalls how a hallway that once felt endless to a small child slowly seemed to shrink with each return visit, a metaphor for a family growing apart in the same house.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
At six it took forever to get to my parents' door
I'd run it at full speed and still feel small
At ten it was three doors and a runner rug
At fourteen I could cross it without looking up

[Verse 2]
It wasn't the hallway, the hallway never moved
It was how much less I wanted to arrive
Somewhere between the rug and the third door
We stopped being a family that walked toward each other

[Chorus]
The hallway got shorter every single year
Not because I grew, because I learned
There's nothing at the end worth running for
The hallway got shorter and I let it

[Verse 3]
I measured it once with a tape on a dare
Nineteen feet exactly, the same as it ever was
But I'll tell you the truth and you can check the math
Some nights it was a mile and some nights it was nothing

[Chorus]
The hallway got shorter every single year
Not because I grew, because I learned
There's nothing at the end worth running for
The hallway got shorter and I let it

[Bridge]
Nineteen feet, three doors, one runner rug gone thin
That's the whole geography of how we drifted
No shouting, no slammed door, just less and less walking
Until the hallway was just a hallway again

[Outro]
At six it took forever, now it takes nothing at all
I'm still standing at the start of it, some nights

title: Letters Pressed with Flowers from the Yard
description: A box of old letters, each one flattening a dried flower picked from the family yard, reveals a parent's private sadness the child never knew to look for until much later.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
I found the box behind the winter coats
String-tied letters, dates in careful pen
A pressed white flower fell out of every fold
Same flower, same spot, the one by the fence

[Verse 2]
I knew that flower, I picked it as a kid
Never knew my mother was pressing them for someone
The letters weren't sent, the stamps still blank
She wrote them and folded a flower and kept them in the dark

[Chorus]
I'm not opening this drawer on the atlas
I'm just marking it exists, that's all I can do
There was a whole country inside her I never got to visit
And the only map of it is pressed flowers and a string

[Verse 3]
I didn't read past the first line of the first one
Some rooms aren't mine to walk through twice
I tied the string back exactly how I found it
And put the box back behind the winter coats

[Chorus]
I'm not opening this drawer on the atlas
I'm just marking it exists, that's all I can do
There was a whole country inside her I never got to visit
And the only map of it is pressed flowers and a string

[Bridge]
Same flower, same fence, same hand that tucked me in at night
Writing letters to nobody under a winter coat
I used to think I knew every room in that house
I didn't know the biggest one was inside a person

[Outro]
String-tied, unsent, a flower in every fold
I'm marking it exists, that's all I'll ever know

title: The Empty Chair at Sunday Dinner
description: A grandmother's chair left in its place at the table for months after her death, set but never filled, captures the family's quiet refusal to admit she was gone.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
Nobody moved the chair from the head of the table
We just set one less plate and left the space
Dad still passed the bread around it like always
Like the gap itself was hungry too

[Verse 2]
I was seven, I didn't understand the rule
I asked once why we don't just push it in
My mother's face did something I had never seen
I never asked again, I just learned to pass the bread

[Chorus]
The empty chair is a room on my atlas
A room with no walls, just an outline in the air
We sat around her absence every Sunday for a year
And called it dinner, and called it fine

[Verse 3]
One day the chair was just gone, no warning, no goodbye
Someone must have carried it down to the basement
I looked for it for months in every closet
Like losing the chair was losing her twice

[Chorus]
The empty chair is a room on my atlas
A room with no walls, just an outline in the air
We sat around her absence every Sunday for a year
And called it dinner, and called it fine

[Bridge]
Pass the bread around the gap, pour the water for one less
Nobody said her name but everyone heard it
That's the strangest grief I ever learned to carry
The kind that sets a place and never says why

[Outro]
I still leave a little space when I set my own table
I don't know who it's for, I just know how

title: The Shed Nobody Was Allowed to Enter
description: A locked backyard shed, off-limits for reasons never explained, becomes the central mystery of the album's grief arc, a literal locked room standing in for a family secret kept from the children.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
Padlock and a chain, even in summer
Even when every other door stood open wide
Dad kept the only key on his own ring
And told us it was full of nothing, full of nothing

[Verse 2]
We used to press our eyes against the gap in the boards
Saw a workbench, a tarp, a shape we couldn't name
Once I heard him in there at two in the morning
Not building, not fixing — just standing, just breathing

[Chorus]
I drew the shed on the atlas as a question mark
I drew it bigger than the house, bigger than the yard
Whatever was nothing took up all the room in him
And none of us were ever told the name of it

[Verse 3]
The lock rusted shut years before he did
We never did open it, not even after
Some maps you finish, some you leave the edge torn
The shed stayed locked, and so, I think, did he

[Chorus]
I drew the shed on the atlas as a question mark
I drew it bigger than the house, bigger than the yard
Whatever was nothing took up all the room in him
And none of us were ever told the name of it

[Bridge]
Padlock and a chain, even in summer
A tarp, a workbench, a sound at two a.m.
I don't need the key now, I don't need the answer
I just needed to know that I wasn't wrong to wonder

[Outro]
Whatever was nothing took up all the room in him
I'm still drawing the question mark, still drawing it

title: The Photograph with the Corner Burned Off
description: A family photo with one corner deliberately burned away leads the narrator to finally learn who was cropped out of the picture and why, the album's central revelation.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
The photo was on the mantel my whole life
Five of us smiling, one corner gone to ash
I always thought a candle got too close
Never thought to ask who used to stand there

[Verse 2]
My aunt finally told me the summer the house sold
There were six of us once, then there were five
A brother before me, eleven months, a fever
They burned him out of every photo they could find

[Chorus]
I found the room I'd been drawing blank my whole life
I found the name underneath the burned-off corner
He's not a ghost, he's not a hole in the atlas
He's the reason the hallway always felt one person short

[Verse 3]
I'm not angry they hid it, I understand it now
Some grief doesn't have room to share a mantel
But I wish I had known I was named for someone
Instead of just carrying his absence and not its shape

[Chorus]
I found the room I'd been drawing blank my whole life
I found the name underneath the burned-off corner
He's not a ghost, he's not a hole in the atlas
He's the reason the hallway always felt one person short

[Bridge]
Eleven months, a fever, a summer nobody named
They kept the photo and burned off just his side
Love that couldn't look at him and couldn't throw him out either
That's the strangest kind of keeping I have ever seen

[Outro]
Five of us smiling, one corner finally has a name
I'm filling it in, I'm filling it in

title: The Fever Summer
description: The narrator imagines the lost summer of 1991, the season their unnamed brother lived and died, reconstructing it from fragments — a heat wave, a hospital parking lot, a casserole dish nobody ever returned.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
Nineteen ninety-one, the hottest June on record
I wasn't born yet but I know the weather report
I know the parking lot had no shade at noon
I know because my mother told me once, just once

[Verse 2]
The neighbors brought casseroles for two months straight
One dish never got returned, it's still in our garage
I used to think it was just a dish nobody claimed
Now I know exactly whose hands carried it over

[Chorus]
I didn't live the fever summer but I inherited the map of it
Every road in our house detours around that June
I'm filling in a season I was never there to see
With a hospital parking lot and a casserole dish and the heat

[Verse 3]
They say he had my father's hands, my mother's stubborn chin
They say the house was quiet for a year after
I grew up in the quiet without knowing it was aftermath
I thought that's just what our house sounded like

[Chorus]
I didn't live the fever summer but I inherited the map of it
Every road in our house detours around that June
I'm filling in a season I was never there to see
With a hospital parking lot and a casserole dish and the heat

[Bridge]
No shade at noon, no air in the ward
A dish in the garage with no name on the lid
I wasn't there, I wasn't there, I wasn't there
But I grew up fluent in the silence it left behind

[Outro]
I'm filling in a season I was never there to see
The heat's still in the walls, the heat's still in me

title: The Name I Was Never Told
description: Learning his brother's name for the first time at thirty years old, the narrator speaks it aloud in the empty house before it's sold, finally giving the hidden grief a shape and a voice.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
The lawyer slid the papers across the table
Next of kin, full names, nothing left blank
There it was, between my father's and my own
A name I had never once heard spoken aloud

[Verse 2]
Thomas. Thomas Alan. Born March, died May
Thirty years I walked past that name in silence
I drove to the empty house before the new owners came
Stood in the kitchen and said it for the first time

[Chorus]
Thomas, I'm mapping you onto the atlas now
Thomas, you get a room, you get a road, you get a name
I'm not angry it took this long to find you
I'm just glad I get to say it before the house is gone

[Verse 3]
The walls didn't answer, the kitchen stayed quiet
But something in my chest unlocked like an old shed door
I said it twice more, just to hear how it fit
Thomas. Thomas. Like he'd always had a chair at the table

[Chorus]
Thomas, I'm mapping you onto the atlas now
Thomas, you get a room, you get a road, you get a name
I'm not angry it took this long to find you
I'm just glad I get to say it before the house is gone

[Bridge]
Thirty years of an outline, thirty years of a question mark
One name on a legal page and the whole shape changes
I carried you without knowing what to call the weight
Now I know, now I know, now I know

[Outro]
Thomas Alan, born March, died May
I'm saying it so the house remembers too

title: Redrawing the Floorplan
description: Now armed with the full truth, the narrator redraws the childhood house from memory one more time, adding the locked shed, the burned photograph, and the missing brother's room as known, named places rather than blank spaces.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
I'm starting the atlas over, the same house, new ink
The shed isn't a question mark anymore, it's just a shed
The burned corner has a name written under it now
Every room I used to draw empty, I'm filling in

[Verse 2]
The hallway's still nineteen feet, I measured it again
But I drew it long this time, the way it felt at six
The drawer still sticks, I drew the sticking too
Some things don't need fixing, they just need to be true

[Chorus]
I'm redrawing the floorplan with everything I know now
No more blank rooms, no more pencil where there should be ink
The grief gets a room and the wonder gets a room
And for the first time they're both on the same page

[Verse 3]
My mother's letters get a drawer of their own this time
Thomas gets the room at the end of the hall
The empty chair gets drawn exactly as empty
Because that's what it was, and that's allowed to stay

[Chorus]
I'm redrawing the floorplan with everything I know now
No more blank rooms, no more pencil where there should be ink
The grief gets a room and the wonder gets a room
And for the first time they're both on the same page

[Bridge]
It's not a happier house, it's just a whole one
It's not a lighter map, it's just a finished one
Every sad room is true and every glad room is true
And the house finally holds both without falling down

[Outro]
I'm redrawing the floorplan, the ink is finally dry
This is the house, this is the whole house, this is mine

filmPrompt: slow aerial drift over a fully illustrated paper floorplan as warm light spreads evenly across every room in sequence, ink lines glowing faintly as if being completed in real time, calm continuous camera movement, warm gold-and-amber palette throughout

title: The Road That Was Always a Circle
description: The narrator realizes the long, complicated route they mapped out of childhood was never a straight line away from home but a circle leading back to understanding and accepting where they started.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
I thought the road went out, away, and further still
Through every city I moved to just to prove I'd left
I kept the atlas in a box under every new bed
Never noticed the roads I drew all curved

[Verse 2]
Twenty years of moving and I'm back on the same street
Not because I failed, because I finally saw the shape
Every road I thought was leading me away from that house
Was just the long way of teaching me how to walk back in

[Chorus]
It was always a circle, never a straight line out
Every city was just a longer way of saying home
I'm not ashamed of how far I went to learn this
I needed the whole map to find the one road that mattered

[Verse 3]
I stand at the end of the street where the house used to be
There's a new one now, different roof, same slope of land
I don't need the house, I needed the understanding
And the road delivered it exactly where it started

[Chorus]
It was always a circle, never a straight line out
Every city was just a longer way of saying home
I'm not ashamed of how far I went to learn this
I needed the whole map to find the one road that mattered

[Bridge]
Every road curves if you draw it long enough
Every distance is just home wearing a disguise
I'm folding up the atlas now, not because I'm finished
But because I finally know which way is back

[Outro]
It was always a circle, I just needed the miles to see it
I'm home, I'm home, the road brought me home

title: The Atlas Closes on Its Own Front Door
description: The album's finale, set at the literal front door of the now-finished memory atlas, where the narrator closes the book of childhood rooms not to forget them but to finally be able to carry them forward, whole and at peace.
lyrics:
[Verse 1]
Last page of the atlas is the front door itself
The one with the loose hinge that we never oiled
I drew it open just a little, not shut all the way
Because that's how doors like that always actually were

[Verse 2]
I used to think finishing the map meant locking it away
Putting the rooms in boxes, never looking back
But every room I drew taught me how to hold it
Not heavier, not lighter, just held, just mine

[Chorus]
I'm closing the atlas on its own front door
Not to forget the house, just to carry it different now
Every sticking drawer, every locked shed, every empty chair
They're not weighing me down, they're just walking with me

[Verse 3]
Somewhere a kid is racing paper boats down a different gutter
Somewhere a globe is wearing thin in someone else's hands
I don't need that house standing to keep what it gave me
I carry the rooms, the rooms don't carry me anymore

[Chorus]
I'm closing the atlas on its own front door
Not to forget the house, just to carry it different now
Every sticking drawer, every locked shed, every empty chair
They're not weighing me down, they're just walking with me

[Bridge]
Loose hinge, thin paint, fourteen steps, one burned corner
A name finally spoken, a road that was a circle
I built this whole map just to learn how to put it down gently
And pick it back up only when I want to

[Outro]
The front door's still open just a little, the way it always was
I'm not walking back in, I'm just glad I know where it is
